payload.formula_text: Audience signals are configured at the asset group level to guide Google's AI targeting. They inform but do not restrict who sees your ads.
payload.formula_explanation: Lists all audience signals configured for Performance Max campaigns. Signals include Customer Match lists, remarketing audiences, and custom intent audiences. These guide Google's machine learning but don't limit reach.
how_to_read: Audience Name shows the signal label from your Google Ads account
Scope indicates whether the signal applies to asset groups or customers
Customer Match audiences (converters, purchasers) typically drive best performance
LAL (Lookalike) audiences expand reach based on high-value customers
Remarketing audiences target users who previously engaged with your site
decisions_answered: What audience signals are configured for PMAX?
Are we using Customer Match lists effectively?
Should we add more first-party audience signals?
How diverse is our audience signal strategy?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows the audience signals configured for your Performance Max campaigns.

Unlike traditional targeting, PMAX audience signals are suggestions to Google's AI, not hard restrictions. They guide the algorithm toward valuable users.

Reading this table: look at the Scope column. Asset Group signals apply to specific ad groups. Customer signals apply account-wide.

The most valuable signals are typically Customer Match lists - your actual converters and purchasers. LAL or Lookalike audiences expand reach based on these high-value customers.

Remarketing audiences target users who've already engaged with your site - they're warmer and more likely to convert.

This answers: What signals are we using? Are we leveraging first-party data effectively? Should we add more Customer Match lists or remarketing segments to improve PMAX targeting?

payload.formula_text:
Daily Spend per Campaign = SUM(SPEND) GROUP BY DATE_DAY, CAMPAIGN_NAME
Top 5 campaigns selected by total spend in the date range
payload.formula_explanation: Shows daily spend for the top 5 Performance Max campaigns over the selected date range. Each campaign is a separate colored line, allowing you to see how budget distributes across campaigns day by day.
how_to_read: Each colored line represents one campaign â€” the legend shows which color maps to which campaign
Click legend items to hide/show individual campaigns for focused comparison
Higher lines indicate campaigns consuming more daily budget
Overlapping lines mean campaigns spend similar amounts on those days
Hover over any date to see exact spend, revenue, clicks, and conversions for each visible campaign
decisions_answered: Which campaign is consuming the most budget day to day?
Are any campaigns spiking or dropping unexpectedly?
How does spend distribution across campaigns change over time?
Should budget be reallocated between campaigns based on trends?
payload.narration_text:
This chart shows daily spend for your top 5 Performance Max campaigns over the selected date range.

Each colored line represents a different campaign. The legend above the chart shows which color maps to which campaign name. You can click legend items to hide or show individual campaigns.

When reading this chart, look for which campaigns dominate spend. If one campaign line is consistently much higher than others, that campaign is consuming most of your budget.

Watch for sudden spikes or drops â€” a campaign suddenly increasing spend might indicate a bidding change or new asset group. A sudden drop could mean the campaign was paused or budget was capped.

This complements the Campaign Performance table, which shows aggregate totals. This chart answers the time dimension: How is each campaign trending day by day?

payload.formula_text:
Campaign ROAS = Campaign Conversion Value / Campaign Spend
Campaign CPA = Campaign Spend / Campaign Conversions
payload.formula_explanation: Displays all Performance Max campaigns with key metrics over the last 30 days. Includes campaign status, bidding strategy, budget, spend, clicks, conversions, conversion value, ROAS, and CPA. Color-coded for quick identification of high and low performers.
how_to_read: Sort by any column to rank campaigns on that metric
Green ROAS values (>=10x) indicate excellent performance
Yellow ROAS (6-10x) is good, red (<6x) needs attention
Low CPA (green) is better - means cheaper customer acquisition
Check Optimization Score column for Google's health assessment
decisions_answered: Which PMAX campaigns are top performers?
Which campaigns need optimization or budget changes?
How do different bidding strategies perform?
Are any campaigns wasting budget with poor ROAS?
payload.narration_text:
This table shows all your Performance Max campaigns with comprehensive metrics.

Key formulas: ROAS is conversion value divided by spend. CPA is spend divided by conversions.

To read this table effectively: click any column header to sort. Green values indicate strong performance - high ROAS above 10x, low CPA below 5 dollars. Red flags struggling campaigns.

Look at the bidding strategy column - compare how Maximize Conversion Value performs versus Maximize Conversions. Check the Optimization Score for Google's own health assessment.

This answers critical questions: Which campaigns are winners? Which need attention? Should you reallocate budget from poor to strong performers? Are certain bidding strategies outperforming others?

payload.formula_text:
Device Spend = SUM(COST_MICROS) / 1,000,000 WHERE DEVICE = [device_type]
Device ROAS = Device Conversion Value / Device Spend
payload.formula_explanation: Aggregates Performance Max spend and conversions by device type (Mobile, Desktop, Tablet, Connected TV). Each device contributes differently to overall performance based on user behavior patterns.
how_to_read: Bar length represents spend allocation - longer bars mean more budget goes to that device
Mobile typically dominates spend due to higher user volume
Hover to see ROAS and CPA for each device - efficiency can vary significantly
Desktop often has higher ROAS despite lower spend due to higher conversion intent
Connected TV spend is typically smaller but growing for brand awareness
decisions_answered: How is PMAX budget distributed across devices?
Which device delivers the best ROAS efficiency?
Should we adjust device targeting based on performance?
Is mobile spend proportional to mobile conversions?
payload.narration_text:
This chart breaks down your Performance Max spend and performance by device type.

The formula groups all campaign stats by device - Mobile, Desktop, Tablet, and Connected TV - then calculates spend and ROAS for each.

Reading this chart: longer bars mean more spend on that device. Mobile usually dominates because that's where most users are. But don't confuse volume with efficiency.

Hover over each bar to see the full picture - ROAS, CPA, and conversion rate. Desktop often shows higher ROAS despite lower spend because desktop users tend to convert with higher intent.

Use this to answer: Which device is most efficient? Is mobile spend justified by its returns? Should you adjust any device targeting?

payload.formula_text:
Spend = SUM(COST_MICROS) / 1,000,000
Revenue = SUM(CONVERSIONS_VALUE)
Clicks = SUM(CLICKS)
Conversions = SUM(CONVERSIONS)
payload.formula_explanation: Tracks four key volume metrics for Performance Max campaigns over the selected date range. Spend and Revenue share the left (currency) axis, while Clicks and Conversions share the right (count) axis. This complements the Performance Trend chart which focuses on efficiency (Spend + ROAS).
how_to_read: Blue line shows daily spend â€” the amount invested each day
Purple line shows daily revenue (conversion value) â€” higher is better
Amber line shows daily clicks â€” traffic volume indicator
Green line shows daily conversions â€” purchase/goal completions
Left axis ($) scales Spend and Revenue; right axis scales Clicks and Conversions
Hover over any date to see exact values for all four metrics
decisions_answered: How do spend and revenue track together over time?
Are clicks converting into actual purchases?
Which days or periods show the highest volume?
Is there a gap between traffic (clicks) and outcomes (conversions)?
payload.narration_text:
This chart tracks four key volume metrics for your Performance Max campaigns over the selected date range.

The blue line shows your daily spend - how much you're investing each day. The purple line shows daily revenue from conversions. These share the left dollar axis.

The amber line tracks daily clicks - your traffic volume. The green line shows conversions - actual purchases or goal completions. These share the right count axis.

When reading this chart, look for how spend and revenue move together. If revenue consistently exceeds spend, your campaigns are profitable. Watch for days where clicks spike but conversions don't follow - that might indicate landing page issues.

This complements the Performance Trend chart above, which shows efficiency (ROAS). This chart answers volume questions: How much am I spending? How much am I earning? How much traffic and how many conversions?

payload.formula_text:
ROAS = Conversion Value / Spend
Daily Spend = SUM(COST_MICROS) / 1,000,000
payload.formula_explanation: This chart tracks daily Performance Max spend and ROAS over 90 days. Spend is the total advertising cost in dollars (converted from micros). ROAS measures how much revenue each dollar of ad spend generates.
how_to_read: Blue area shows daily spend amount - higher peaks indicate days with more budget deployed
Green dashed line shows ROAS trend - values above 8.0x are considered excellent
The red horizontal line marks the 8.0x ROAS target benchmark
Look for correlation between spend increases and ROAS changes to identify efficiency patterns
Hover over any point to see exact values for that date
decisions_answered: Is Performance Max ROAS improving or declining over time?
How does increased spend affect returns?
When did significant performance changes occur?
Are we consistently meeting our ROAS targets?
payload.narration_text:
This performance trend chart tracks your Performance Max campaign metrics over the last 90 days.

The blue shaded area represents daily spend - the amount you're investing in PMAX each day. Higher peaks mean more budget was deployed that day.

The green dashed line shows your ROAS - Return on Ad Spend. This is calculated by dividing conversion value by spend. The red horizontal line marks your 8.0x target.

When reading this chart, look for patterns: Does ROAS drop when you increase spend? That might indicate diminishing returns. Do certain periods consistently outperform others? That could reveal seasonal patterns.

This chart helps you understand if PMAX efficiency is improving, whether you're hitting targets, and when major performance shifts occurred.

payload.formula_text:
Quarterly ROAS = Quarter Conversion Value / Quarter Spend
Quarter = DATE_TRUNC(DATE, QUARTER)
payload.formula_explanation: Aggregates Performance Max performance by calendar quarter to reveal seasonal patterns and long-term trends. The 8.0x target line helps assess whether quarters meet profitability goals. Q1 typically shows highest ROAS for seasonal businesses.
how_to_read: Each bar represents one quarter's ROAS performance
Red dashed line marks the 8.0x ROAS target
Bars above the line indicate profitable quarters
Q1 often peaks for seasonal businesses (planting season for garden supplies)
Look for year-over-year patterns by comparing same quarters across years
decisions_answered: Which quarters deliver the best PMAX returns?
Is ROAS improving or declining over time?
How does seasonality affect PMAX performance?
Should we adjust budgets based on quarterly patterns?
payload.narration_text:
This bar chart shows your Performance Max ROAS by quarter, revealing seasonal patterns and long-term trends.

The formula aggregates conversion value and spend by calendar quarter, then calculates ROAS. The red dashed line marks your 8.0x target.

Reading this chart: bars above the line mean profitable quarters. Q1 2025 at 11.8x was exceptional - likely due to peak planting season if you're in garden supplies.

Notice how Q4 and recent quarters show lower ROAS. This could be increased competition during holidays, or a sign that PMAX needs optimization.

Compare same quarters across years - if Q1 2025 beat Q1 2024, that's real improvement. If it's worse, you need to understand why.

This answers: Which seasons are best? Is performance trending up or down? Should you adjust seasonal budgets?
